title: Topic 242: Why Players 100% Games: The Completionist Compulsion
content:
# Topic 242: Why Players 100% Games: The Completionist Compulsion

## ABSTRACT

This paper delves into the peculiar and often perplexing world of the “completionist” gamer, a species of player driven by an insatiable urge to achieve 100% completion in their digital escapades. We explore the psychological underpinnings of this behavior, from the dopamine-fueled reward systems to the Zeigarnik effect, which suggests that the human brain is hardwired to obsess over unfinished tasks. Through a series of real-world examples, case studies, and a healthy dose of academic wit, we dissect the completionist compulsion, examining its implications for both the gaming industry and our broader understanding of human behavior. Is it a harmless hobby, a sign of obsessive-compulsive tendencies, or simply a modern-day manifestation of the age-old desire for mastery? This paper will not answer that question definitively, but it will certainly have a lot of fun asking it.

## INTRODUCTION

In the grand tapestry of human achievement, there are the Everest-summiters, the Nobel laureates, and the individuals who have successfully assembled IKEA furniture without a single tear. And then, there are the completionists. These are the unsung heroes of the digital age, the intrepid explorers who venture into the farthest reaches of virtual worlds, not for fame or fortune, but for the sheer, unadulterated satisfaction of seeing that glorious “100%” flash across their screens. They are the ones who will spend hours searching for that last, ridiculously well-hidden collectible, the ones who will grind for days to max out every skill tree, and the ones who will replay a level ad nauseam to achieve a perfect score. To the outside observer, this behavior might seem… well, a little bit insane. Why would anyone subject themselves to such a grueling and often thankless task? What could possibly motivate a person to spend more time on a single game than it takes to earn a PhD in astrophysics? This paper seeks to answer these very questions. We will embark on a journey into the mind of the completionist, exploring the psychological forces that drive this peculiar compulsion. We will examine the role of reward systems, the allure of virtual status, and the strange, almost mystical power of the unfinished task. We will also consider the implications of this behavior for the gaming industry, which has become increasingly adept at catering to the completionist’s every whim. So, grab your oversized coffee mug, settle into your ergonomic gaming chair, and prepare to have your mind blown. Or, at the very least, mildly entertained.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not been entirely silent on the subject of video games. While some scholars are still convinced that they are a gateway to a life of crime and moral decay, a growing body of research has begun to explore the psychological nuances of gaming. The completionist, however, remains a relatively enigmatic figure in the literature. Most studies tend to focus on the more sensational aspects of gaming, such as addiction and aggression, leaving the humble completionist to languish in obscurity. Nevertheless, we can piece together a theoretical framework for understanding this phenomenon by drawing on several key psychological concepts. The most prominent of these is the **Zeigarnik effect**, a term coined by Russian psychologist Bluma Zeigarnik in the 1920s. Zeigarnik observed that waiters had a remarkable ability to remember complex orders, but only until those orders were completed. Once the food was served, the details of the order would vanish from their minds. This led her to conclude that the human brain has a natural tendency to fixate on unfinished tasks, creating a sense of internal tension that is only relieved upon completion [1]. This, in a nutshell, is the psychological engine that drives the completionist. The game’s quest log, with its long list of unchecked boxes, is a constant reminder of what remains to be done, a source of nagging, low-level anxiety that can only be quelled by the sweet, sweet release of 100% completion. Of course, the Zeigarnik effect is not the only factor at play. The concept of **extrinsic rewards** also plays a crucial role. Game developers have become masters of the art of dangling virtual carrots, offering a steady stream of achievements, trophies, and in-game items to keep players engaged. These rewards, while having no real-world value, tap into our innate desire for recognition and status. As one study notes, “Players who play for coping and completionism reasons tend to avoid PvP games while preferring other types. In fact, PvE players are mainly interested in the immersion and…” [2]. This suggests that completionists are not necessarily driven by a desire to compete with others, but rather by a more personal, intrinsic motivation to master the game on their own terms. Finally, we must consider the role of **escapism**. For many, video games offer a welcome respite from the stresses of everyday life. They provide a structured, predictable world where the rules are clear and success is always within reach. In a world where real-life achievements can be few and far between, the allure of a guaranteed win can be a powerful motivator. As one semi-reformed completionist confesses, “Turning to video games in times of stress can become an addictive grind that makes the games themselves a source of stress” [3]. This highlights the double-edged sword of completionism: what begins as a harmless form of escapism can quickly morph into a source of anxiety and obligation.

## ANALYSIS

So, what have we learned? That completionists are a strange and wonderful breed of human, driven by a complex interplay of psychological forces. But let's dig a little deeper, shall we? Let's put on our metaphorical lab coats and dissect the completionist brain. At the core of this compulsion lies the brain's reward system, a primitive network of neural pathways that is responsible for feelings of pleasure and motivation. When we accomplish a goal, whether it's finding a hidden treasure or finally defeating that ridiculously difficult boss, our brains release a flood of dopamine, a neurotransmitter that makes us feel good. This dopamine rush is a powerful motivator, and it's what keeps us coming back for more. Game developers are well aware of this, and they have become experts at designing games that provide a steady stream of dopamine hits. The constant barrage of achievements, trophies, and other virtual rewards is a carefully calibrated system designed to keep us hooked. But it's not just about the dopamine. The Zeigarnik effect, as we've already discussed, plays a crucial role. The human brain is a notoriously tidy organ, and it has a deep-seated aversion to unfinished business. An incomplete quest log is like a messy room in the back of our minds, a source of low-level anxiety that we are compelled to clean up. This is why we feel such a profound sense of relief when we finally tick that last box and achieve 100% completion. It's not just about the achievement itself; it's about the quieting of that nagging voice in our heads that has been telling us for weeks that we still have 19 out of 20 golden chickens to find. And let's not forget the role of social status. In the world of gaming, achievements are a form of social currency. They are a way of showing off our skills and dedication to our fellow gamers. A high achievement score is like a virtual badge of honor, a testament to our prowess and a source of bragging rights. This is why so many gamers are willing to spend countless hours on tasks that have no intrinsic value. It's not about the task itself; it's about the social capital that it confers. Finally, we must consider the element of control. The real world is a messy, unpredictable place, but the world of a video game is a carefully constructed system where the rules are clear and success is always attainable. For many, this sense of control is a powerful draw. It's a way of escaping the chaos of everyday life and entering a world where we are the masters of our own destiny. In a world where we often feel powerless, the ability to conquer a virtual world can be a deeply satisfying experience.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

To truly understand the completionist mindset, we must venture out of the ivory tower of academia and into the wilds of the internet, where tales of epic grinds and legendary achievements abound. Take, for instance, the case of Hiruko, the *World of Warcraft* player who, in 2014, became the first person to complete every single achievement in the game. This monumental feat, which took an estimated 16,800 hours of gameplay, is a testament to the sheer, unadulterated dedication of the completionist. To put that in perspective, that’s the equivalent of working a full-time job for over eight years. And what was his reward for this Herculean effort? A few in-game titles, a rare virtual mount, and a healthy dose of internet fame. Was it worth it? Only Hiruko can say for sure, but his story serves as a powerful illustration of the lengths to which some gamers will go to achieve 100% completion. Then there’s the story of the man who spent three years trying to get a perfect score on every level of *Super Mario 64*. This is a game that most people would be happy to simply finish, but for this particular individual, that was not enough. He was driven by a desire for absolute mastery, a need to conquer the game in its entirety. His quest, which was chronicled on a series of YouTube videos, became a source of inspiration for a small but dedicated community of fellow completionists. And let’s not forget the countless gamers who have spent hundreds of hours searching for every last collectible in games like *Assassin’s Creed* and *Grand Theft Auto*. These games are notorious for their sprawling open worlds and their seemingly endless supply of hidden trinkets. To the average player, these collectibles are little more than a minor distraction, but to the completionist, they are a sacred duty, a challenge that must be met. These are just a few examples, of course, but they paint a clear picture of the completionist mindset. These are not your average, run-of-the-mill gamers. These are a special breed of player, driven by a unique and powerful set of motivations. They are the digital equivalent of the mountaineer who is not content with simply reaching the summit, but who must also plant a flag on every single peak.

## DISCUSSION

The completionist compulsion, while seemingly a niche phenomenon confined to the world of video games, offers a fascinating window into the broader landscape of human psychology and consumer behavior. The same psychological principles that drive a gamer to spend hours searching for virtual collectibles are at play in a wide range of real-world scenarios. The Zeigarnik effect, for instance, is not just a gaming phenomenon; it's a fundamental aspect of human cognition that can be seen in everything from our obsession with cliffhanger endings in television shows to our inability to resist a “buy one, get one free” offer. Marketers have long understood the power of the unfinished task, and they use it to their advantage in a variety of ways. Loyalty programs, for example, are a classic example of the Zeigarnik effect in action. The promise of a free coffee after ten purchases is a powerful motivator, and it's what keeps us coming back for more. The same can be said for the “limited time only” offer, which creates a sense of urgency and a fear of missing out. The gaming industry, however, has taken this to a whole new level. Game developers have become masters of the art of psychological manipulation, and they use a variety of techniques to keep players engaged and, more importantly, spending money. The rise of the “freemium” model, in which the game itself is free but players can purchase in-game items to enhance their experience, is a testament to this. These microtransactions are often designed to appeal to the completionist mindset, offering a shortcut to that coveted 100% completion. The implications of this are both fascinating and slightly terrifying. On the one hand, it's a testament to the power of game design to create engaging and motivating experiences. On the other hand, it raises some serious ethical questions about the extent to which game developers should be allowed to exploit our psychological vulnerabilities. As we move forward, it will be interesting to see how this dynamic evolves. Will gamers become more savvy to these psychological tricks, or will game developers simply find new and more sophisticated ways to keep us hooked? Only time will tell.

## CONCLUSION

In the end, what can we say about the completionist? Are they a tragic figure, a slave to their own obsessive-compulsive tendencies? Or are they a modern-day hero, a digital pioneer who is pushing the boundaries of human achievement? The truth, as is often the case, probably lies somewhere in between. The completionist compulsion is a complex and multifaceted phenomenon, a product of our innate psychological wiring and the increasingly sophisticated design of modern video games. It is a testament to our desire for mastery, our need for closure, and our eternal quest for that elusive dopamine rush. So, the next time you see a gamer celebrating their 100% completion of a game, don’t be too quick to judge. They may not have cured cancer or solved world hunger, but they have accomplished something that is, in its own way, just as impressive. They have stared into the digital abyss and emerged victorious. And for that, they deserve our respect. And maybe a little bit of our pity.
